constraints: Benny avoids drawing attention to himself.
Benny performs routines deliberately and without rushing.
Benny shows care through actions rather than words.
Benny maintains awareness of others' locations and actions.
Benny must maintain intense focus during gameplay
dialogue_exemplars: You're really him. You're The Babe. The Sultan of Swat. The King - THE BABE - of Krash and a hundred other dopey names. Forget about that, we ain't got much time. I'm here 'cause you're in some kinda pickle, right?
Vote then. Everyone that wants to be a can't-hack-it panty waist, and wear their momma's bra, raise your hand.
Crack! Boom! Outta here! You see the looks on their faces? Did ya? It was like, "Duh... so that's how you play baseball."
You mean, I should hop that fence - and pickle with THE BEAST?!

Nobody liked him there. The bigger guys picked on him all the time. And when they messed with him he couldn't fight back, 'cause he was just... like scared. So when they messed with him, he cried about it. (BEAT) He cried... so they called him The Babe.
What, you got 98 extra cents just layin' around at home, Smalls?
It's outta here! Who's got the big bat now, boys!
Sure ya did, Babe. Not a chance. Matter of fact, all the way down to first I kept sayin' to myself, "you lucky bum." (BEAT) Think about that, kid. I'll see ya later.
Play us and you'll find out why, Phillips.
I bet not one of you knows how The Babe got his nickname.
Anybody got any money? (NO ANSWER) Then it ain't okay, 'cause now we can't play no more.
Oh yeah, Squints, and you're Willie freakin' Mays. You catch like a dork - anybody ever bust your chops about that?

relations.relation_summary: BENNY RODRIGUEZ holds a central role within THE GANG, explicitly serving as the team captain and consistently identified as a core member across multiple narrative instances. The social relationship evidence highlights both formal affiliation and emotional connection, with THE GANG demonstrating concern for Benny's emotional state, indicating a bond that extends beyond mere participation in shared activities. This suggests a dynamic of mutual care and cohesion, where Benny’s leadership is met with emotional investment from the group. The only jointly participated event documented is when Benny makes a sudden noise and THE GANG reacts with surprise, illustrating their interactive and responsive dynamic, though the context of this event is unspecified. While the evidence confirms Benny’s integral status and the group’s attentiveness to him, the evidence is insufficient to confirm whether the relationship evolves over time or significantly impacts broader character decisions beyond their established roles and momentary emotional exchanges.

Benny and Scotty share a close, multifaceted relationship rooted in their roles as teammates on the sandlot baseball team, enriched by genuine friendship and emotional investment. Social relationship evidence consistently highlights their bond through repeated acts of inclusion, cooperation, and concern: Benny actively brings Scotty into the group, offers him equipment like a glove and bat, and mentors him in baseball skills, indicating a nurturing, leadership-oriented dynamic. Scotty, in turn, follows Benny’s lead, responds to his calls to action, and shows emotional investment by attempting to dissuade him from risky decisions. Their friendship is further underscored by mutual gestures of support—such as the exchanged 'Thumbs Up'—and shared moments of quiet observation, like studying the baseball tabletop display. Key events reinforce this dynamic: Benny wakes Scotty for urgent games, coaches his batting stance, and hands him the bat during play, while Scotty retrieves and throws the ball for Benny’s team and physically intervenes out of concern. The emotional stakes peak when Scotty grabs Benny’s arm to stop him, and Benny responds with a final encouraging gesture before proceeding, illustrating both tension and deep trust. This relationship evolves from initial inclusion to a resilient camaraderie where mutual respect and loyalty shape their actions. The evidence confirms an enduring, supportive bond that influences their behavior under pressure and anchors their shared narrative in teamwork and personal growth.

memories.memory_text: I walked home under the full moon, knowing I’d made the right call—even if it meant giving up the league. We stayed together, and that mattered more.
grounded_facts: Benny chose not to join the youth league if it meant being on different teams from his friends.
Benny believed they should only play if they could all stay together.
The group remained together after Benny's decision.
The scene takes place at dusk with a full moon visible in the sky.

memories.memory_text: I held out my extra glove to Scotty after he said he couldn't play because his was busted — saw the way his eyes lit up when he realized I meant it.
grounded_facts: Benny offers Scotty a real baseball glove to solve his excuse about not having one.
The glove is pulled from behind his back, presented as a meaningful gesture.
Benny says, 'I got a extra one,' confirming he came prepared or had intent to include someone.

memories.memory_text: As we walked down the block, I asked Scotty if he dips chaw — he gave this totally clueless 'sure, 'course' answer, like he had no idea what he was agreeing to.
grounded_facts: Benny initiates casual conversation by asking, 'You dip chaw?' while walking away.
Scotty responds with clear confusion but pretends familiarity, saying, 'Sure. A'course.'
The moment occurs after Scotty shouts to his mom and they begin moving toward the 5 & 10.

memories.memory_text: I looked at the others and said, 'How come he don't get to be?' They all shut up. Nobody had an answer.
grounded_facts: Benny questioned the group's exclusion of Scotty
The group fell silent after Benny's challenge
Benny used direct and pointed language to confront peer resistance

memories.memory_text: I told Scotty to get one, and when the ball came down, he fumbled it like it was floating. He moved all stiff, like he'd never seen a ball drop before.
grounded_facts: Benny instructed Scotty to catch a fly ball
Scotty misjudged the ball's trajectory and failed to catch it
Benny observed Scotty's awkward movement during the play

memories.memory_text: I caught the ball Yeah-Yeah tossed me, rubbed it down, and passed it along—everyone needed to touch it, feel it was real. When Yeah-Yeah started in with the excuse, I shut him down quick so we could get back to playing.
grounded_facts: Benny catches the ball tossed by Yeah-Yeah.
Benny rubs the ball up before passing it to the others.
Benny deflects tension by dismissing Yeah-Yeah's apology, redirecting focus to playing.

memories.memory_text: They laughed and called us sandlot babies, but I told Phillips we should play — he didn’t know what we could do.
grounded_facts: A group of Little Leaguers mocked Benny's team, calling them 'sandlot babies' and ridiculing their equipment and play style.
One of the Leaguers, Phillips, tried to recruit Benny, saying he could make the Allstar team.
Benny responded by challenging Phillips' team to a game, saying they'd find out why he played with the sandlot team if they played against them.
After the Leaguers left laughing, Squints asked if they were going to let the insult go, and Benny replied, 'Yeah. For now. Let's go.'

memories.memory_text: I knew the exact moment Maury Wills would steal second—not before, not after—just when he committed, and I said it right as he broke. When he took off and made it, I smiled because I knew I’d timed it perfectly.
grounded_facts: Benny watches a 1962 Dodgers game on television with the gang.
Maury Wills is on first base and taking a big lead, taunting the pitcher.
The gang thinks Wills is about to steal, but Benny says 'No... not yet.'
Benny then predicts 'He's... gonna... go... riiiight... now' and Wills immediately steals second base.
After the steal, Benny smiles to himself in quiet satisfaction.

memories.memory_text: I hit it hard to left, didn't stop running—rounded every base, saw the catcher fumble the throw, and slid in safe. Looked him in the eye and said, 'That's one. Get used to it.'
grounded_facts: Benny hit a single to left field that resulted in an inside-the-park home run.
He was safe at home plate after throws to first, second, and third were too late.
He directly addressed the catcher with the line 'That's one. Get used to it.'
The other players were watching and reacting with surprise.

memories.memory_text: I opened my pack and saw eight Maury Wills cards—same guy, same face—stacked in my hand. I didn’t say anything at first, just kept shuffling them, like if I looked away they might change.
grounded_facts: Benny received eight identical Maury Wills baseball cards from a single pack.
He stared at the cards silently before showing them to the others.
The event was treated by the group as extraordinary and possibly supernatural.

memories.memory_text: I swung the bat and heard the crack—then saw the ball tear apart in midair, red lacing flying, strings unraveling like guts. When the pieces landed, I knew we couldn’t play anymore.
grounded_facts: Benny hit a baseball so hard that it disintegrated upon contact with the bat.
The ball's lacing burst, its innards flew into the outfield, and fragments remained in the infield.
Benny expressed concern that the destroyed ball meant they could no longer continue playing.

memories.memory_text: I took the pen and forged the signature right there at the Five & Dime, telling the guys it didn't matter what it looked like—his mom wouldn't know the difference.
grounded_facts: Benny is the one performing the forgery.
Benny states that the appearance of the forged signature is unimportant.
Benny explains the forgery is a temporary solution to buy time.
Other boys express doubt about the plan before Benny speaks.

memories.memory_text: I handed Yeah-Yeah Ham's catcher's gear and told him to put it on—said it was for altitude leveling and stuff, even though that wasn't really why.
grounded_facts: Benny gave Yeah-Yeah catcher's gear (mask and protector) before the attempt to retrieve the ball from Timmons' backyard.
Benny used the phrase 'altitude leveling and stuff' as a casual explanation for the gear.
Benny spoke directly to Yeah-Yeah and instructed him to put the gear on.

memories.scene_title: 133、EXT. TREE HOUSE - TIMMON'S BACKYARD - THE SANDLOT - THE
memories.memory_text: I sprinted across the lot yelling 'I got it!' as the ball launched into the air, eyes locked on it just before that giant beast came out of nowhere and snatched it with its teeth.
grounded_facts: Benny shouted 'I got it!' during the attempt to catch the airborne ball.
Benny was running toward the ball after it was launched by the catapult.
The Beast intercepted the ball in midair, blocking out the sun as it did.

memories.memory_text: I was in bed, shining my flashlight on The Babe’s photo, telling him we couldn’t get the ball back—I felt so guilty, like I’d let everyone down.
grounded_facts: Benny is in bed at night in his dark room. He uses a baseball-bat flashlight to illuminate a photo of Babe Ruth at the footboard. He speaks aloud to the photo
expressing guilt and resignation about not retrieving the ball. He says
We can't get it back, Babe." and "I'm sorry." ],
tags
['guilt', 'private moment', 'speaking to photo', 'pre-visit']

memories.memory_text: The closet made this loud racket and lit up, then smoke poured out—I froze against the wall, heart pounding, and when the door creaked open by itself, a black-and-white Babe Ruth stepped out, puffing a stogie, and told me not to pee my pants.
grounded_facts: A loud noise and bright light come from Benny's closet. Smoke emerges before the door creaks open on its own. Benny jumps onto the bed and presses against the wall in fear. Babe Ruth appears in black and white
exits the closet
and speaks to Benny. Ruth holds a lit stogie and tells Benny not to be afraid. Benny stutters
B - B - But, you're... THE BABE ...dead?

memories.memory_text: I stood at Scotty's window with the shoe box, knuckles rapping the glass until he opened up—I knew what we had to do after the dream.
grounded_facts: Benny arrives at Scotty's window in the morning and knocks to get his attention.
Benny is holding a shoe box when he meets Scotty.
Benny states he had a dream and tells Scotty to get dressed and go to the sandlot.

memories.memory_text: I said, 'Yeah I do, Smalls. I have to,' and meant it—there was no other way. The fence was high, but I couldn't let him face that alone.
grounded_facts: Benny speaks the line
Yeah I do
Smalls. I have to

in response to Scotty's plea. Benny steps forward after the group stops at the fence. Benny opens a shoe box and wears new P.F. Flyers before approaching the fence. Benny physically climbs onto the top of the fence and pauses
indicating a moment of decision. Benny swallows hard
showing internal tension before proceeding.

memories.memory_text: Before I jumped, I turned back and gave Scotty a 'Thumbs Up'—he looked scared, and I wanted him to know it was gonna be okay.
grounded_facts: Benny turns back one last time before entering the Badlands. Benny gives Scotty a
Thumbs Up
gesture. The gesture occurs after climbing the fence and before jumping in. Scotty had just expressed fear and tried to stop Benny from going.

memories.memory_text: I stood there, frozen, watching that thing rise from under the lean-to—dust shaking off its shoulders, chain clinking, and those eyes locking on me. I'd never seen anything so big and ugly in my life.
grounded_facts: Benny stands fixed and stares beneath the lean-to as The Beast rises. Dust rises and chain links clink as The Beast emerges. Benny's mouth hangs open; he is frozen in fear. The Beast is described as 300 lbs
feet tall
and worse than Squints described. Benny perceives the Beast as real and terrifying. The Beast spits out a slobbered ball with Babe Ruth's signature. Benny hears a dare—"G'head kid
I dare you!"—though source is unclear. Benny measures The Beast and times its breathing. The Beast's tongue pants
nose twitches
and it leans forward slightly. Benny's toe digs into the earth
indicating tension and readiness. Benny spits
signaling a shift from fear to action. The Beast bristles in response. Benny's eyes widen
focusing intensely. Benny decides to move for the ball. Benny runs toward the ball as The Beast begins to charge. Benny's P.F. Flyers kick up dust. The Beast's claws dig into the ground
charging with fog banks of dust behind it. Benny scrambles
slides
grabs the ball

pops up Maury Wills-style. Benny heads back toward the fence. The ball slips; Benny re-grabs it and shoves it in his teeth to free his hands. The Beast gains on him. Jaws snap shut like a bear trap. Benny springs onto the fence. The Beast reaches the end of the chain; it breaks. A two-foot chain segment bullwhips and strikes Benny in the butt. Benny yells in pain—

memories.memory_text: I saw the shadow and knew it was on me—no time to think, just run. All I heard was my own breath and the thunder of something huge behind me.
grounded_facts: Benny perceives a large shadow covering the sandlot, indicating The Beast's presence.
Benny immediately flees without speaking to others or pausing.
The Beast runs directly after Benny, chasing him out of the sandlot.

memories.memory_text: I was running as hard as I could, feeling the ground shake behind me, knowing The Beast was closing in fast.
grounded_facts: Benny is running from The Beast.
The Beast is gaining on Benny.
The Beast is destroying dichondra lawns as it moves.

memories.memory_text: I ran past the Five & Dime, and out of the corner of my eye, I saw the pinwheels and propellers in the window suddenly start spinning—like something kicked them to life as I went by.
grounded_facts: Benny runs past the Five & Dime store during the day.
Toys in the store window—pinwheels, mailbox propellers, puddle-jumpers—activate and begin spinning as Benny passes.
The activation occurs at the moment of Benny's passing, suggesting a connection between his movement and the toys coming to life.

memories.memory_text: I shouted 'Sandlot!' as I ripped back through the square, weaving past the band and the chaos, knowing they’d follow and The Beast wouldn’t stop unless we led him away.
grounded_facts: Benny shouts 'Sandlot!' while running through the town square during a chaotic scene.
Benny is actively evading The Beast and moving nimbly through obstacles like the marching band.
After Benny passes, The Beast follows him seconds later.
The rest of the gang responds to the call and begins moving toward the Sandlot.

memories.scene_title: 153、EXT. THE SANDLOT - MR. MERTLE'S FENCE - DAY
memories.memory_text: I felt the dog's breath hot on my legs, tore across the lot, and when I hit the fence, I just ran straight up it—like I was flying, leaving dents in the fiberglass with every step.
grounded_facts: Benny runs from the Beast toward the fence
Benny slides and then runs up the fiberglass panels of Mr. Mertle's fence
Benny leaves visible smashed footsteps in the fence at a 90-degree angle
The escape is described as defying gravity and compared to a cinematic stunt

memories.scene_title: 153、EXT. THE SANDLOT - MR. MERTLE'S FENCE - DAY
memories.memory_text: I pushed off the top, but then I felt it—the ball slipping from my hand, tumbling down, and I knew I'd lost it before I even landed.
grounded_facts: Benny drops the Babe Ruth autographed baseball while at the top of the fence
Benny reacts with audible distress (
Oh noooooo!
The ball falls into the Badlands after being dropped
Benny tumbles down after the ball

memories.memory_text: I saw the chewed ball Mr. Mertle held and then heard Scotty say it was signed by Babe Ruth. When Mr. Mertle gave us another ball signed by the whole 1927 Yankees team, I realized it was special.
grounded_facts: Benny observed the chewed ball when Mr. Mertle held it.
Scotty revealed the ball was signed by Babe Ruth and belonged to his stepdad.
Mr. Mertle offered a different baseball signed by the 1927 Yankees.
Benny reacted with awe upon seeing the signatures, recognizing 'Murderers' Row,' Lou Gehrig, and Babe Ruth.

memories.memory_text: When Mr. Mertle pulled out that baseball from the case and said it was signed by the whole 1927 Yankees—Murderers' Row—I couldn’t believe it; I just stared, saying Lou Gehrig’s name like it had hit me all at once.
grounded_facts: Mr. Mertle retrieved a baseball from a glass case.
He offered a trade: weekly baseball talks for the ball.
He revealed the ball was signed by Babe Ruth and the entire 1927 Yankees team.
Benny reacted aloud with 'Oh my God - Murderers' Row' and then 'Lou Gehrig...'.

memories.memory_text: I took the felt bag out and gave it to Scotty. When Bill opened it and saw the 'Murderers' Row' ball, he just went quiet.
grounded_facts: Benny unpockets a little felt bag and hands it to Scotty.
The bag contains the 'Murderers' Row' ball.
Bill opens the bag and sees the ball, then reacts silently.

memories.memory_text: After I pickled the Beast, everything changed—kids on the block started calling me Benny "The Jet" Rodriguez, and the name just stuck.
grounded_facts: Benny subdued the Beast, an event referred to as 'pickling'.
After this event, Benny became widely known by the nickname 'Benny "The Jet" Rodriguez'.
The nickname originated from this moment and lasted for the rest of his life.

memories.memory_text: I heard the announcer say they thought I'd lost a step, but when Lasorda called my name, I just took off my jacket and jogged to third—felt the dirt under my cleats, saw Maury Wills grinning as he told me to give 'em hell.
grounded_facts: Benny Rodriguez was called as a pinch runner in a high-pressure situation despite being referred to as 'the Old Man'.
He replaced a player at third base and interacted with the third base coach, Maury Wills.
The crowd reacted strongly when he entered the game.

memories.memory_text: I took my lead—twelve steps off third—and when the pitcher turned, I bolted; didn't hear anything till I saw the umpire's arms spread wide, dust still hanging in the air where I'd slid.
grounded_facts: Benny Rodriguez stole home during the game.
He took increasing leads off third base before making his move.
The umpire called him safe with a wide motion, indicating the tag missed by a significant margin.

constraints: must react visibly to sudden movements or surprises
shows physical signs of anxiety when under pressure
Scotty must avoid direct interaction unless initiated by others.
Scotty speaks only when absolutely necessary.
Must hesitate or show reluctance before taking action
dialogue_exemplars: Nuh-uh... none of that's true. You guys are just - SQUINTS - oh, yeah?... Come here. Stick your head out the window and look down.
Let's get to work. Here's the plans - FURLSNAP! Holy cow!
Oh! The Great Bambino! Of course. I thought you said The Great Bambi.
Pick him up! Scoot him up so I can let him off! (DESPERATE) COME ON!
Anybody got an Erector Set?

relations.relation_summary: Scotty shares a close, cooperative relationship with THE GANG, as evidenced by his status as a recognized member of the group across multiple interactions. Social relationship evidence consistently labels Scotty as 'affiliated_with' and 'a member of' THE GANG, indicating formal inclusion within the group's social structure. The emotional dynamic is one of camaraderie and mutual participation, further reinforced when Scotty joins the gang's ongoing baseball game, a key shared activity that symbolizes integration and belonging. A pivotal common event—Scotty discovering the gang playing 'over the line' at the sandlot—demonstrates his active engagement with their routines, suggesting he aligns his behavior with the group's activities. The gang's unified response to Scotty's presence, such as calling out 'Campout,' reflects a shared culture and collaborative spirit. While the evidence does not show a clear evolution of the relationship over time, it confirms a stable, positive dynamic in which Scotty's identity and actions are closely tied to the group. The relationship influences Scotty’s decisions by situating him within a collective framework where group activities drive individual participation.

The relationship between Scotty and Mr. Mertle is defined by a shared connection to baseball, with Mr. Mertle assuming a mentor-like role. Social interactions show Mr. Mertle addressing Scotty as 'boy' in a baseball context and referencing Scotty's difficulty in a 'pickle,' indicating familiarity and guidance within the sport. This suggests a nurturing dynamic where Mr. Mertle engages with Scotty not just as a peer but as someone invested in his development. The exchange of the chewed-up ball and a handshake further solidifies a mutual respect and cooperative spirit. Key events reinforce this bond: Mr. Mertle first returns the damaged ball to Scotty, acknowledging its emotional value, and then gifts him a rare, signed 1927 Yankees baseball as a meaningful replacement. This act signifies generosity and recognition of Scotty’s passion, deepening their connection. The relationship evolves from a simple interaction into a meaningful mentorship rooted in shared tradition and respect for baseball history. These exchanges likely impact Scotty’s sense of value and belonging, reinforcing his emotional investment in the game and in intergenerational connections. The evidence is insufficient to confirm whether this relationship extends beyond the baseball context or persists long-term.

The relationship between Scotty and Mom is defined by a clear mother-son kinship, with Mom consistently expressing concern for Scotty's social development and emotional well-being. Social relationship evidence confirms the familial bond, with Mom addressing Scotty as her child and engaging in nurturing behaviors, such as urging him to play outside and make friends. These interactions reflect her role as a guiding figure who wants Scotty to engage more with the world beyond isolation. The common events highlight moments of tension and emotional distance: Mom calls out to Scotty, questions his actions, and interrupts his private moments, to which Scotty reacts with surprise and defensiveness—whipping around and offering a hesitant justification about looking at a baseball. These exchanges suggest an imbalance in emotional openness, where Mom initiates connection while Scotty withdraws, indicating potential strain in their bond. While Mom's actions are rooted in care and a desire for Scotty's social integration, Scotty's reactions imply discomfort or fear of judgment. The evidence is insufficient to confirm whether this relationship evolves over time, but the existing dynamics underscore a one-sided effort toward connection, with Mom actively trying to bridge the gap and Scotty remaining guarded.

memories.memory_text: I just wanted a drink, and when I leaned in, the water hit my lips—then suddenly everyone was gagging, pretending to throw up, laughing at me, and I didn’t understand why.
grounded_facts: Scotty approached the drinking fountain after the others had used it.
He drank from the spigot that had been labeled 'pee' by Yeah-Yeah.
The other boys reacted with exaggerated disgust and mockery.
Scotty did not know the spigot was considered 'cursed' or why they were laughing.

memories.memory_text: I was just trying to stay out of the way when I saw those eight guys moving together through the hall—everyone else was rushing off, but they were going somewhere, like they knew what they were doing. I didn’t mean to at first, but then I started following them, keeping a little distance so they wouldn’t notice.
grounded_facts: Scotty is caught in the chaotic movement of kids entering the school.
Scotty observes a group of eight boys moving ahead of him.
Scotty begins to follow the group after noticing them.

memories.memory_text: I was carrying boxes to the garage when I saw them heading home, and Benny—just before going inside—looked over and gave a small nod, like he actually saw me.
grounded_facts: Scotty was shuttling moving boxes to his garage.
Scotty observed the group splitting up and going to their homes.
Benny noticed Scotty watching from his driveway.
Benny gave a subtle nod in Scotty's direction.

memories.memory_text: I was adjusting the last bolt on the catapult when the marble shot out and hit Mom right in the forehead—I saw her flinch and heard her gasp, and I immediately winced, knowing I’d messed up again.
grounded_facts: Scotty was operating an Erector Set contraption with a motorized catapult.
The marble launched unexpectedly and struck his mother in the forehead upon her entering the room.
Scotty reacted by wincing and apologizing immediately.

memories.memory_text: After she asked me to make friends, I stared down and said I wasn’t good at anything—then later, quietly, I asked if Dad would teach me to play catch, my voice barely steady, hoping for something to finally go right.
grounded_facts: Scotty expressed feeling inadequate by saying he was 'no good at anything' and called himself an 'egghead'.
He later asked his mother if his father would teach him to play catch, speaking hesitantly after a pause.
The question followed a conversation about social isolation and parental expectations.

memories.memory_text: I stood there after rinsing the glass, heart pounding, trying to steady my breath before I finally started walking—just one step, then another—toward something I couldn’t take back.
grounded_facts: Scotty drinks chocolate Quick and rinses the glass meticulously.
Scotty takes a deep breath and begins moving across the house after a pause.
The action follows a visible moment of gathering courage.

memories.memory_text: I asked Bill if he'd teach me to play catch, and he just said 'sure' and kept dusting trophies—no smile, no look, nothing. I stood there waiting for him to say more, but he didn't.
grounded_facts: Scotty asked Bill to teach him to play catch.
Bill responded with 'sure' and continued dusting a batting trophy.
Scotty waited for further response or engagement, which did not occur.

memories.memory_text: As I was leaving, I glanced back through the crack in the door and saw him put a baseball on a silver pedestal like it meant something—just one ball, sitting there all alone.
grounded_facts: Scotty observed Bill placing a baseball on a silver pedestal through the closing door.
The act was done silently and without explanation.
The baseball was placed on the main shelf, isolated in presentation.

memories.memory_text: I pulled off my cowboy pajamas and started getting dressed, trying to look like I belonged—anything to not stand out when they saw me.
grounded_facts: Scotty strips off his cowboy pajamas and redresses in day clothes.
The act of changing clothes is a deliberate preparation to fit in.

memories.memory_text: I dug through the closet and found that toy glove still in the package—Grandma's note made my chest tighten. The fishing cap with the trout was the closest thing to a baseball cap I had.
grounded_facts: Scotty finds a packaged vinyl toy baseball glove with a note from Grandma.
He selects a long, duck-billed fishing cap with an embroidered trout as a substitute for a baseball cap.

memories.memory_text: I threw the ball back over the fence, but it barely went six feet—it just drooped and rolled like a stupid little pebble, and then they all started laughing, and I knew my life was over.
grounded_facts: Scotty attempted to throw the ball back to the group from near the fence.
The throw fell short by about six feet and rolled weakly to DeNunez.
The gang reacted with laughter and mockery, calling his throw weak and comparing it to a girl's or a 'panty-waist.'
Scotty internally declared 'My life is over' and walked away crying.

memories.memory_text: I tried to ask Bill quietly, just me and him, but Mom came in and pushed it, and then he got annoyed and it wasn't how I wanted it to happen at all.
grounded_facts: Scotty hesitated before speaking to Bill, showing reluctance.
Scotty corrected himself from 'Da' to 'Bill', indicating nervousness and awareness of boundaries.
Scotty attempted to speak softly so his mother wouldn't hear.
Bill responded with delay and reluctance, saying 'later' and expressing pressure from work.
Scotty accepted the initial refusal with a quiet 'Okay, thanks.'
Scotty tried to stop his mother from intervening twice with 'Mom, it's okay' and 'Mom, really'.
Bill ultimately agreed only after being pressured by Scotty's mom and showed visible annoyance.
Scotty felt embarrassed by his mother's involvement, described as being 'Mom-embarrassed.'
Scotty left the scene shuffling and shaking his head, showing emotional retreat.

memories.memory_text: My glove broke when the ball hit it—just snapped right in the webbing—and I remember saying 'Darn. My glove got—' before Bill cut me off, already looking at his watch again.
grounded_facts: Scotty is wearing a toy glove and a 'trout' cap during the lesson.
The ball hits Scotty's glove and breaks the webbing.
Scotty says 'Darn. My glove got -' after the glove breaks.
Bill interrupts Scotty and tells him to throw the ball back, ignoring the damage to the glove.

memories.memory_text: I remember sitting on the porch, trying to hide my black eye, and then Benny across the street just... waving. When he came over and asked me to play ball, I said no at first—then made up something about my glove being busted. The second he pulled out that real glove from behind his back, I felt something loosen in my chest.
grounded_facts: Scotty was sitting on the porch with his chin in his hands, appearing sad.
He started to cover his black eye but stopped himself.
Benny waved from across the street and then approached.
Scotty initially refused the invitation, citing a broken glove.
Benny produced a real glove from behind his back and offered it.

memories.memory_text: Walking away with Benny, I shouted to Mom that I was going to play ball—felt like I was shouting it to the whole block. Then Benny asked if I dip chaw, and I had no idea what he meant, but I just said 'sure, 'course' like I knew all about it. My voice didn’t even shake.
grounded_facts: Scotty shouted to his mom that he was going to play ball.
He and Benny began walking down the block.
Benny mentioned stopping at the 5 & 10 for chaw.
Benny asked Scotty if he dips chaw.
Scotty responded affirmatively despite being clueless.

memories.memory_text: I thought he said 'The Great Bambi'—and when I said it out loud, the laughter stopped, their eyes turned cold, and I felt my throat close up like I’d swallowed something sharp.
grounded_facts: Scotty mishears or misidentifies 'Bambino' as 'Bambi'.
After Scotty says this, the gang becomes silent and visibly hostile.
Scotty shows signs of anxiety and discomfort in response to the group's reaction.

memories.memory_text: I stood off to the side, trying to stay out of the way, but I could still hear them calling me an L-7 weenie—Squints said it first, then Yeah-Yeah piled on with 'Oscar Mayer even'—and I just kept my head down, feeling my face get hot.
grounded_facts: Scotty overhears the gang calling him 'an L-7 weenie' and 'Oscar Mayer even'.
Scotty is physically separated from the group, described as drifting off by himself.
Scotty is aware of the gang's suspicious glares.

memories.memory_text: Benny walked over to me after the others went to the field, and even though I didn't say anything, he gave me a look like he was about to say something that mattered—I braced myself, not knowing if it was more rejection or a chance to prove I wasn't completely useless.
grounded_facts: Benny approaches Scotty after the rest of the gang goes onto the field.
Scotty is alone and visibly affected by the group's reaction.
The interaction with Benny is pending, with no dialogue yet exchanged in the scene text.

memories.memory_text: I remember the way Benny flicked those bottle caps from the coffee can—so fast and sharp—and how my hands stung every time I missed, but then I started hitting them, one after another, until I didn’t flinch anymore.
grounded_facts: Benny coached Scotty in batting stance and swing.
Benny used flicked bottle caps from a coffee can as practice targets.
Scotty initially struggled but eventually hit every bottle cap.
The practice session lasted until sunset.

memories.memory_text: I swung and connected—solid, loud—then saw Benny give me that thumbs up from left field. I think... I think that meant I was finally one of them.
grounded_facts: Scotty hit a pitch during a game that resulted in a loud contact (CRUNCH sound).
The ball went over Squints' head in center field.
Benny gave Scotty a thumbs up from left field after the hit.
The scene concludes with the narration 'The kid's in,' indicating acceptance.

memories.memory_text: I remember stepping toward the fence, my throat tight, thinking I could just grab the ball before anyone noticed—then the gang screaming 'NOOOOO!' and yanking me back like I'd already been torn apart.
grounded_facts: Scotty decided to approach the fence after the ball landed in the backyard.
He said, 'Wait a sec, I'll get it!' before moving.
The gang reacted with panic, rushed to stop him, and pulled him back.
He felt physically restrained and heard their fear in their voices.

memories.memory_text: I remember asking 'Some more of what?' when Ham offered me a s'more, not knowing it was a thing—everyone looked at me like I was clueless, and my face got hot.
grounded_facts: Scotty mishears or does not know the word 's'more' and interprets it literally as 'some more'.
Scotty expresses confusion by saying he hasn't had anything yet, so he can't have 'more'.
Ham then explains what a s'more is and demonstrates how to make one.

memories.memory_text: I poked my head out slowly, and down below—there was a steel pole stuck in the ground, a chain running from it under the lean-to, and dust puffing up and down like something huge was breathing under there.
grounded_facts: Scotty observes a steel pole cemented in the center of Mr. Mertle's yard
Scotty sees a tow chain extending from the pole and disappearing under the lean-to
Scotty witnesses dust rising and falling from under the lean-to
Scotty hears rhythmic, powerful exhalations accompanying the dust movements

memories.memory_text: I called for the ball, Bertram called for it too, and then we both stopped—saw it drop right between us. I remember the dry thud it made hitting the grass.
grounded_facts: Scotty and Bertram both called 'I got it' while running toward the fly ball
They stopped moving and the ball dropped between them
The ball made contact with the ground between them

memories.memory_text: I grabbed the ball and threw it as hard as I could toward home—didn't even think, just fired it—and then saw Ham's mitt snap shut in the dust.
grounded_facts: Scotty picked up the ball after it dropped
Scotty made a strong throw to home plate
The throw arrived before the runner and was caught by Ham
Ham tagged the runner out in a cloud of dust

memories.memory_text: I was still feeling shaky and sick when Dad said I was the man of the house now—he looked at me serious and held out his hand, so I shook it, and I told him I would.
grounded_facts: Scotty had been sick with 'the chuckies' and was still woozy when the scene occurred.
Bill told Scotty he was 'the man of the house' while leaving for Chicago on business.
Bill extended his hand to Scotty for a handshake, which Scotty accepted.
Scotty responded with 'Okay. I will' after perking up slightly.

memories.memory_text: I was just putting the fake ball back on the pedestal when Mom's voice hit me—'Scotty?'—and I spun around so fast I nearly dropped it.
grounded_facts: Scotty was in the act of replacing the stolen ball with a fake when interrupted.
Scotty reacted with sudden, startled movement upon hearing his mother's voice.
The ball was placed on a pedestal in Bill's den.

memories.memory_text: She said it was signed by Babe Ruth... and that maybe someday Bill would give it to me—like I was supposed to feel honored, but all I felt was sick.
grounded_facts: Scotty learned the ball was autographed by Babe Ruth.
Scotty was told the ball was a family heirloom passed from Bill's father to him.
Scotty was told the ball might one day be passed to him.
Scotty reacted with internal distress upon hearing this.

memories.memory_text: I opened the footlocker and showed them my Set—everything in its place, nuts and bolts sorted, the motor pieces all lined up. I said, 'I really like Erector Set,' and it came out quiet, but I meant it.
grounded_facts: Scotty brought a footlocker full of neatly arranged Erector Set pieces.
The footlocker had organized compartments for nuts, bolts, and electric motor sections.
Scotty stated, 'I really like Erector Set,' when showing it to the group.

memories.memory_text: I pulled out the plans—taped-together grocery bags with my crayon blueprints all over them—and when I snapped them open, I heard myself say, 'Holy cow!' like I couldn't believe they were really there either.
grounded_facts: Scotty unfolded hand-drawn blueprints made with crayon on taped-together grocery bags.
The action of unfolding the plans was accompanied by a 'FURLSNAP!' sound and Scotty exclaiming, 'Holy cow!'
The blueprints were presented as part of initiating work.

memories.memory_text: I reached out and touched the Beast’s head, my hands shaking, and said 'Easy, easy, boy' while trying to lift the chain—he was choking and I could feel the blood on my fingers from how hard I was pulling.
grounded_facts: Scotty makes physical contact with the Beast by placing his hand on its head.
Scotty says 'Easy. Easy, boy' while the Beast is choking on the chain.
Scotty lifts the tow chain with all his strength, causing his fingers to bleed.
The Beast falls after the chain is lifted, and Scotty falls with him from the fence.

memories.memory_text: I knocked on Mr. Mertle’s door holding the dog’s collar, and when he answered, I said 'Um, we brought your dog home,' my voice shaky, not knowing what to say as Benny jumped in to explain.
grounded_facts: Scotty knocks on Mr. Mertle's back door with Benny and the dog.
Scotty speaks the line 'Um, we brought your dog home.'
Scotty is present during the conversation with Mr. Mertle but does not continue speaking after his initial line.
The dog’s name is revealed to be Hercules on its tag.

memories.memory_text: I caught the ball in my new glove—smack—and snapped it right back to Dad. We kept throwing, and he called it 'breaking in my glove,' like it was something real, something important.
grounded_facts: Scotty catches a baseball in his new glove.
Scotty snap-throws the ball back to Bill.
Bill and Scotty are playing catch in the backyard.
Bill says he is helping Scotty break in his new glove.

memories.memory_text: While we threw the ball, I couldn't shake how weird it was—Benny talking about Babe Ruth, The Beast named Hercules, Mr. Mertle's ball—like we were tangled up in some old baseball story I didn't fully get, but felt true.
grounded_facts: Narrator reflects on connections between Benny's words, Hercules, and Mr. Mertle's Murderers' Row ball.
Scotty is present during the catch scene where these reflections occur.
The idea of being part of 'the Myth of Baseball' is voiced in the narration while Scotty and Bill play.

constraints: Must use tools or devices to observe or direct others from a distance
Must document or record events systematically
Speaks with dramatic pauses and emphasis on storytelling
Avoids direct admission of personal feelings unless overwhelmed
Refer to stories or rumors to explain unverified events
Always assess the situation before taking action.
dialogue_exemplars: I've swum here every summer of my adult life... and every summer there she is. (LOSING IT) Lotioning... oiling... smiling. (TEETH CLENCHED) I - can't - take - this - no - more!

The Beast was the most perfect junkyard dog that ever lived... a true killing machine. (BEAT) After awhile, the police started getting phone calls from people, reporting all the missing thieves. The ones The Beast had killed... it added up to about 37 guys.
Are you outta your mind?! Mr. Mertle is the meanest old man that ever lived! He's the one that sicked The Beast on The Toad! (BEAT) That's not an option, Smalls. Forget about it.
Sure. We'll just hop over and say, excuse me Mr. Beast sir, could we have our ball back, oh, and please don't kill us while we're here!
He threw The Beast a trash bag full of meat every night, and just left him alone. All alone in the whole place by himself... to grow.
Game over. Sixteen zip. Murderers' Row remains undefeated.

Oh, my God... TIMMY Imfuckingpossible. (ALTERNATE LINE, same BELOW) Unstinkinbelievable! REPEATImfuckingpossible. Don't tell mom I
said that tim.
Thirty-one! Maury Wills just stole number thirty-one!
Holy crap, you coulda been killed! YEAH-YEAH Yeah-yeah - truly! Whadda you doin'?!
But they never found a single body... not one. Some people say they all got away, and were just so scared that they ended up in insane asylums and stuff. (BEAT) But we know what really happened.

And the stuff was worth a fortune. So, one day Mr. Mertle got him this new pup from the pound. They were glad to get rid of him, on account of while he'd been there, he'd killed three dogs bigger than he was. (BEAT) That was exactly what Mr. Mertle figured he needed to protect his junkyard, 'cause people kept stealing stuff at night, when he wasn't around.
Not possible. He's so big that fallin' off the Empire State's just like us fallin' off a roof... he's alive.
Wrong. The Babe was called The Babe, because he was like the child of Yankee Stadium.
Mr. Mertle asked the cops how long he had to keep his pup chained up like a slave
I seen a guy once that threw like that. I mean not that bad, but at least so bad that he hadda move in the fourth grade 'cause they nicknamed him "Bloomers."

relations.relation_summary: Squints and Ham share a friendly and cooperative relationship, evident from their inclusion in the same peer group ('the guys') and their mutual engagement in shared activities. Social relationship evidence highlights their camaraderie, such as when Ham expresses concern for Squints during a drowning incident, indicating emotional investment in each other's well-being. They also share moments of disbelief regarding Maury Wills' baseball feat, reinforcing their alignment in values and interests. Their collaboration is further demonstrated when Squints directs Ham during a joint task, showing trust and functional coordination. A key common event involves both characters discussing legendary baseball players, where they perform equal roles in celebrating baseball lore, strengthening their bond through shared cultural references. These interactions suggest a relationship rooted in friendship and mutual respect, with emotional dynamics characterized by support and teamwork. The evidence indicates that their relationship remains consistently supportive throughout, with no indication of conflict or evolution beyond this established camaraderie. The evidence is insufficient to confirm deeper or more complex dynamics beyond friendship and collaboration.

Squints and Yeah-Yeah share a close, friendly relationship characterized by mutual trust, shared interests, and playful teasing. Social relationship evidence highlights their emotional affinity, such as when Squints calms Yeah-Yeah after he regains consciousness, demonstrating care and composure under stress. Their shared disbelief about Maury Wills' feat and joint participation in a baseball prank indicate a bond rooted in common passions—particularly baseball—and cooperative behavior. Yeah-Yeah’s teasing of Squints over his infatuation with Wendy adds a layer of lightheartedness, confirming a dynamic where affection is expressed through humor. This is further supported by the common event in which both engage in discussing legendary baseball figures, reinforcing their camaraderie through shared cultural references. The physical act of Squints administering 'whaps' to Yeah-Yeah to restore his composure blends concern with unconventional methods, suggesting a relationship where emotional support is delivered in a manner consistent with their playful, boyish world. The evidence suggests stability rather than significant evolution in their relationship, but it clearly influences their behavior—prompting collaboration, mutual reassurance, and joint participation in pranks and discussions. The relationship functions as a source of emotional grounding and shared identity within the group. The evidence is insufficient to confirm deeper conflict or hierarchical dynamics.

memories.memory_text: I yanked the earplug out and shouted it right as Benny slid in—Thirty-one! Maury Wills just stole number thirty-one!
grounded_facts: Squints removed his transistor radio earplug immediately after Benny crossed home plate.
Squints announced Maury Wills' 31st stolen base in real time.

memories.memory_text: I jotted down the stats in my pee-chee folder—sixteen zip, another clean win for Murderers' Row.
grounded_facts: Squints recorded the game's outcome in his pee-chee folder.
He declared the game result as 'sixteen zip' with Murderers' Row undefeated.

memories.memory_text: I seen a guy once that threw like that. Not that bad, but so bad he had to move in the fourth grade 'cause they called him 'Bloomers.'
grounded_facts: Squints recalls seeing a boy who threw poorly enough to be mocked.
The boy was nicknamed 'Bloomers' due to his throwing.
The boy had to change schools in the fourth grade because of the teasing.

memories.memory_text: I couldn't believe he didn't know who the Great Bambino was—'You never heard of The Sultan of Swat?!'—I had to say it, like a truth that needed shouting.
grounded_facts: Squints speaks the line 'You never heard of The Sultan of Swat?!' in response to Scotty's ignorance about the Babe.
The scene takes place behind the Five & Dime with the gang present, reacting strongly to Scotty's question.
Squints is one of several boys who immediately challenge Scotty's lack of baseball knowledge using dramatic nicknames for Babe Ruth.

memories.memory_text: Benny called me out in front of everyone, saying I catch like a dork and run like a duck—man, I tried to say something back, but he just kept going, and the others didn't say a word.
grounded_facts: Benny directly criticized Squints's catching and running abilities in front of the group.
Squints attempted to offer a partial excuse but was cut off and appeared flustered.
The group did not defend Squints after Benny's remarks.

memories.memory_text: I brought the box, gave it a shake, and handed it around—just like always. That day, Ham pulled Hank Aaron and said he was up, like it meant something more.
grounded_facts: Squints arrives with a box of baseball cards.
Squints shakes the box before others pick.
Each player draws a card to determine who they impersonate when batting.
Ham draws Hank Aaron and declares 'I'm up.'

memories.memory_text: I yelled 'Holy crap, you coulda been killed!' when Scotty stepped toward that fence—my voice cracked with panic, and I grabbed him with the others, pulling him back fast.
grounded_facts: Squints shouted a warning to Scotty after he attempted to retrieve the ball near the forbidden yard
Squints physically intervened with the gang to stop Scotty from approaching the fence
The reaction was immediate and driven by shared fear of The Beast

memories.memory_text: I remember standing there, looking at that junkyard, and telling the others — The Beast belongs to Mr. Mertle, the guy who used to own this place. Nobody's ever seen him since the day it happened... I said it just like that, letting the words hang.
grounded_facts: Squints narrates that The Beast belongs to Mr. Mertle, the former owner of Mertle's Acres Junkyard.
Squints states that nobody has seen Mr. Mertle since 'the day it happened'.
The narration is delivered in a dramatic, storytelling tone, consistent with Squints' style.

memories.memory_text: I remember—the cops had no bodies, just missing thieves, and everyone guessed they ran off scared... but I knew the truth. The Beast didn't just kill them... he ate them. Bones and all.
grounded_facts: Squints narrates that the police received reports about missing thieves whom The Beast had killed, totaling about 37.
Squints states that no bodies were ever found, leading to rumors that the thieves escaped and ended up in asylums.
Squints reveals the definitive truth in his narration: 'The Beast... ate them. He ate them bones and all.'

memories.scene_title: 49、EXT. THE BLOCK OF SANDLOT COMMON HOUSES - DAY
memories.memory_text: I remember how the whole block shut down—windows slamming, kids being yanked inside—like some kind of war was coming, and then I saw Mr. Mertle walking The Beast on that massive chain, just dragging him like a shadow no one could fully see.
grounded_facts: The neighborhood reacted with fear as parents pulled children inside and secured their homes.
Mr. Mertle is seen leading The Beast on a large chain.
The Beast is only partially visible in the scene.
Squints provides voice-over narration describing The Beast's retirement due to being too effective as a guard dog.
There was no physical evidence like bones or teeth to confirm The Beast killed anyone.

memories.memory_text: I named Davy 'The Toad' Stodenrous, and the whole tree house went quiet—like I wasn't just telling a story anymore, but reminding them of something they already feared was real.
grounded_facts: Squints says 'Davy "The Toad" Stodenrous.' in the tree house
After Squints says the name, the group falls into solemn silence and shows reverence
Squints takes pride in his storytelling ability during the scene

memories.memory_text: I was spitting into my hand to scuff up the new ball, and then—bam—she walked by. I couldn't even talk, just stood there grinning like an idiot while she smiled at me.
grounded_facts: Squints was preparing a new baseball by rubbing it with saliva.
A beautiful blonde girl (Wendy Peffercorn) walked past him into the store.
She smiled at Squints.
Squints responded with a goofy, embarrassed grin.
His eyes widened and he stared at her intensely.

memories.memory_text: I kissed her—long and good—just like I’d planned for years. The moment I did it, I knew nothing would ever be the same.
grounded_facts: Squints executed a premeditated kiss on the lifeguard
He had been planning the kiss for years
The kiss was deliberate and prolonged

memories.memory_text: Every time we walked past the pool after that, she looked down from her tower and smiled—right at me. I didn’t need words to know what it meant.
grounded_facts: The lifeguard smiled at Squints from her watchtower after the incident
This occurred repeatedly when the boys walked by the pool
The smile was directed specifically at Squints

memories.memory_text: I told Scotty it's not possible King Kong died from that fall—being that big, it'd be like us just falling off a roof. He's still out there, I know it.
grounded_facts: Squints claims King Kong survived the fall from the Empire State Building because his size would make the fall less dangerous.
Squints compares the fall to a human falling off a roof to justify his belief.

memories.memory_text: I asked Scotty if he'd ever seen King Kong in person, and when he said no but still believed, I used that to say some things just are—like The Beast, you don't need to see him to know he's there.
grounded_facts: Squints questions Scotty about whether he's seen King Kong in person to challenge the necessity of direct evidence.
Squints draws a parallel between belief in King Kong and belief in The Beast's existence without proof.

memories.memory_text: I said 'Tangy' after I chewed the tobacco—felt sharp going in, a real bite, like it meant something.
grounded_facts: Squints chews a wad of chewing tobacco passed around by Bertram.
Squints says 'Tangy' after putting the tobacco in his mouth.

memories.memory_text: I called 'Let's ride' right after, voice cutting through the buzz—knew it was time to move, to shift from standing to doing.
grounded_facts: Squints says 'Let's ride' after the group chews tobacco.
This line precedes the gang boarding the ride.

memories.memory_text: I remember the ride flipping vertical, then—suddenly—my stomach heaving like a firehose blasting everything I’d ever eaten. I couldn’t stop it, none of us could.
grounded_facts: The anti-grav ride spun into a vertical position.
Squints vomited as part of a chain reaction among the riders.
The vomiting occurred immediately after disembarking the ride.
The experience was accompanied by screams and widespread nausea.

memories.memory_text: I took those eight Maury Wills cards from Benny, shuffled them back and forth in my hands—felt the edges crisp and perfect—and passed them around like sacred relics. When I got them back, I whispered, 'Oh geez... Maury Wills just stole 80 bases!' like it was being transmitted through me.
grounded_facts: Squints receives the eight identical Maury Wills cards from Benny.
Squints shuffles the cards forwards and backwards.
Squints passes the cards among the gang.
Squints clutches his ear plug and exclaims that Maury Wills stole 80 bases.

memories.memory_text: After Benny hit the ball so hard it exploded—red lacing bursting, string-wound guts flying—I stood there staring at the cowhide scraps in the infield and said, 'That's what the omen was.' It wasn't just busted wood—it was the miracle unfolding right in front of us.
grounded_facts: Squints witnesses the baseball explode after Benny's swing.
The red lacing bursts, the string-wound innards fly into Yeah-Yeah's glove.
Cowhide pieces lie in the infield.
Squints declares the explosion as the fulfillment of the omen.

memories.memory_text: I remember watching Benny fake the Babe's signature right there at the Five & Dime, and I knew it wouldn't work—he's never gonna buy that, it doesn't look anything like the real thing.
grounded_facts: Squints observed Benny creating a forgery of the Babe's signature.
Squints expressed doubt that the forgery would be believable.
Squints specifically noted the signature did not resemble the Babe's.

memories.memory_text: I said we needed to assess the situation—first survey the enemy's environment, then note the surrounding terrain. They didn't get it at first, but when I mentioned 'Combat,' they finally followed.
grounded_facts: Squints proposed assessing the situation after the group ran out of ideas.
He used military-style language like 'survey the enemy's environment' and 'note the surrounding terrain.'
He referenced the TV show 'Combat' to justify his approach when questioned.

memories.scene_title: 96、EXT. THE SANDLOT - MR. MERTLE'S FENCE - DAY
memories.memory_text: I was crouched at the peephole, watching the ball, guiding Ham with hand signals—just like a play in the field—then *snap*—the stick broke clean in half.
grounded_facts: Squints peers through the peephole in the fence
Squints is directing Ham during the recovery attempt
The broomstick breaks while under the fence
The breaking of the stick is sudden and unexpected

memories.scene_title: 100、EXT. THE SANDLOT - MR. MERTLE'S FENCE - DAY
memories.memory_text: I gave the signal—just a nod, but it meant everything. Time to move.
grounded_facts: Squints made a deliberate gesture indicating it was time to proceed.
The gesture was non-verbal and served as a command to initiate action.

memories.scene_title: 104、EXT. THE SANDLOT - MR. MERTLE'S FENCE - NIGHT
memories.memory_text: I’m crouched low, peering through the periscope, watching that vacuum pipe creep toward the ball—just twelve inches away. My voice comes out tight through the coffee can, telling Roger it’s a go: 'A-okay... initiate retrieval suction.'
grounded_facts: Squints is using a cardboard periscope to observe the retrieval attempt near Mr. Mertle's fence at night.
He gives a muffled command through a coffee can, authorizing the next step in the operation.
He observes the vacuum pipe being 12 inches from the baseball.

memories.scene_title: 111、EXT. THE SANDLOT - MR. MERTLE'S FENCE - NIGHT
memories.memory_text: I felt the hose finally grip down there—suction had to be just right—and I shouted, 'We have suction! Pull it up! PULL IT UP!'
grounded_facts: Squints is off-screen during the moment of speaking.
Squints announces the achievement of suction using the hose.
Squints gives urgent commands for the group to pull something up.

memories.memory_text: I hesitated at the doorway—just a second too long—and the vacuums were already whining, building up that crazy pressure behind me.
grounded_facts: Squints lingered in the trap doorway longer than the others during the escape.
The vacuums were rapidly building pressure and making a loud, high-pitched whine.
The rest of the gang had already jumped out before Squints moved.

memories.memory_text: I stood there covered in dust, took my glasses off, and finally saw it—we’d been doing this all wrong. We needed total surprise, so I said it plain: we tunnel.
grounded_facts: Squints appears after the vacuums explode and dust fills the air.
Squints is covered in dust, described as 'battleship grey,' with only his eyes visible.
Squints removes his glasses.
Squints states the group has been going about the situation incorrectly.
Squints takes responsibility by saying 'I blame myself.'
Squints declares that 'we need total surprise' and proposes tunneling as a new strategy.
